title: Dog Green Mucus from Nostrils: Causes & When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Learn what green nasal discharge in dogs means, the urgency level for veterinary care, and how providing a photo can help your dog get diagnosed.
content_html:
What is Green Nasal Discharge?

Green or yellow mucus from a dog's nose, medically known as purulent nasal discharge, is a sign that the body is fighting an active infection or inflammation. Unlike clear discharge, which can be caused by simple allergies or excitement, colored mucus indicates the presence of white blood cells and cellular debris. Common causes include bacterial or fungal infections, canine influenza, foreign objects (like grass seeds) lodged in the nasal passage, or even advanced dental disease where an infection has moved from the tooth root into the sinuses.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for green nasal discharge is Medium. While it is usually not a life-threatening emergency that requires a visit to an ER at midnight, it is also not a condition that will resolve on its own. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. However, if the discharge is accompanied by extreme lethargy, a refusal to eat, or labored breathing, you should seek veterinary care immediately, as these can be signs that a respiratory infection has progressed to pneumonia.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo or a short video of your dog while the discharge is visible can be incredibly helpful for your veterinarian. A photo allows the vet to see the exact color, thickness, and volume of the mucus, which can change throughout the day. Crucially, it helps the vet determine if the discharge is unilateral (one nostril) or bilateral (both nostrils). Discharge from only one side often suggests a localized issue like a foreign body or a tooth root abscess, while discharge from both sides often points toward a systemic infection or a virus.

faqs.question: Can I use human nose drops on my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human nasal sprays or decongestants on dogs unless specifically directed by your veterinarian. Many human medications contain ingredients that are toxic to pets or can cause dangerous increases in heart rate and blood pressure.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose the cause?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely start with a physical exam and may recommend diagnostic steps such as blood work, nasal swabs for bacterial culture, or X-rays of the snout and teeth to look for the underlying cause of the infection.

faqs.question: Is green mucus in dogs contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. If the mucus is due to a viral or bacterial respiratory infection like Kennel Cough or Canine Flu, it can be highly contagious to other dogs. It is best to keep your dog isolated from other pets until you have a diagnosis.
